question_text:
Cracking the Safe

There is a safe protected by a password. The password is a sequence of n digits where each digit can be in the range [0, k - 1]. The safe has a peculiar way of checking the password. When you enter in a sequence, it checks the most recent n digits that were entered each time you type a digit.

For example, the correct password is "345" and you enter in "012345":

  • After typing 0, the most recent 3 digits is "0", which is incorrect.
  • After typing 1, the most recent 3 digits is "01", which is incorrect.
  • After typing 2, the most recent 3 digits is "012", which is incorrect.
  • After typing 3, the most recent 3 digits is "123", which is incorrect.
  • After typing 4, the most recent 3 digits is "234", which is incorrect.
  • After typing 5, the most recent 3 digits is "345", which is correct and the safe unlocks.

Return any string of minimum length that will unlock the safe at some point of entering it.
input_format: A single line containing two space-separated integers, `n` and `k`.
output_format: A single line containing the minimum length string that cracks the safe.
explanation:
This problem perfectly maps to finding an Eulerian path (specifically a De Bruijn sequence) on a directed graph. In a De Bruijn sequence, we uniquely traverse every possible password combination compactly. The nodes of our graph will be sequences of length `n-1`, and the edges represent the appended `k` character. Walking over an edge from one `n-1` node to another effectively simulates typing one digit on the safe. 
Since an Eulerian Circuit exists on this graph, we can find it via a Depth First Search (DFS). We start with a string of `n-1` zeros. At each node, we try to append all characters from `k-1` down to `0`. If appending a certain character forms an unvisited edge (a unique combination of length `n`), we mark it as visited and DFS recursively. Post-traversal, we append the character to our sequence result. Due to backtracking post-order recursion, reversing the generated result at the end paired with our initial `n-1` zeros gives us our minimum length string cracking the safe optimally.
